content.text: When an agentic AI system misbehaves at 2 AM, the on-call engineer should not be debugging from first principles. A well-maintained troubleshooting runbook turns an unfamiliar failure into a structured investigation with clear steps, decision points, and escalation paths. For generative AI systems — where failures range from hallucinations to runaway loops to vendor API outages — runbooks require regular maintenance because the failure modes themselves evolve with every model update and feature release.
What It Is
An
agent troubleshooting runbook

is a structured, executable document that guides an on-call engineer through diagnosing and resolving a specific class of failure in an agentic AI system. Each runbook covers one symptom category — LLM model failures, tool integration failures, or agent behaviour issues — and provides a decision tree of diagnostic steps, commands to run, data to collect, and escalation criteria. Runbook maintenance is the ongoing process of keeping these documents accurate, discoverable, and effective as the system and its failure modes change.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI failures are novel and non-obvious: a hallucination looks like a successful API response, a runaway loop looks like high traffic, and a prompt injection looks like unexpected user behaviour.
Mean time to resolution (MTTR) correlates directly with how quickly engineers can narrow down the failure class — runbooks encode that decision logic so it does not have to be rediscovered each time.
On-call engineers are often not the engineers who built the system — runbooks transfer expertise to whoever is available.
Model updates, new tool integrations, and feature additions create new failure modes that the existing runbooks do not cover — maintenance keeps the runbooks ahead of the system.
Post-incident reviews generate institutional knowledge about real failure scenarios that should be codified in runbooks immediately, while the details are fresh.
Benefits
Faster MTTR: a runbook halves the time-to-diagnosis by eliminating the search for the right commands and logs.
Consistent incident response: every engineer follows the same diagnostic flow, reducing variability in resolution quality.
Reduced on-call burden: junior engineers can handle a wider range of incidents independently without escalating to senior engineers for basic diagnosis.
Institutional memory: runbooks encode lessons from past incidents that would otherwise live only in incident post-mortems and engineers' memories.
Regulatory evidence: a maintained runbook library demonstrates to auditors that the organization has a systematic approach to AI system reliability.
Trade-offs
Runbook maintenance is ongoing work — an unmaintained runbook for an agentic system can be worse than no runbook if it sends engineers down incorrect diagnostic paths.
Overly prescriptive runbooks remove engineer judgment in situations where the runbook does not quite match the failure — good runbooks prescribe a framework, not a script.
Runbooks do not cover novel failure modes — the first occurrence of a new failure class requires detective work that no runbook can replace.
Quarterly fire drills consume engineer time but are essential for verifying that runbooks are executable — without drills, runbooks rot silently.
How It Works

A runbook library for an agentic AI system is organized around failure symptom categories, with a router at the top that maps observed symptoms to the correct runbook. Within each runbook, a decision tree guides the engineer through log collection, API health checks, and configuration inspection, with branch points based on what they find. Each branch ends in either a resolution step or an escalation trigger.
Runbook index / router:
A top-level document that maps incident alert names and symptom descriptions to specific runbooks.
LLM / model runbooks:
Cover hallucinations, context window exceeded, token budget overage, content filter refusals, and model version mismatches.
Tool / integration runbooks:
Cover API timeouts, rate limit errors (429), tool call schema mismatches, broken retrieval pipelines, expired auth tokens, and downstream database failures.
Agent behaviour runbooks:
Cover infinite loops, wrong tool selection, instruction override attempts (prompt injection), memory or state corruption, and unexpected plan divergence.
Maintenance loop:

After every incident, the on-call engineer opens a PR to add or update the relevant runbook with what they learned. Changes are peer-reviewed, versioned with a dated changelog, published, and validated in the next quarterly fire drill.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Agent troubleshooting runbook architecture: incident triggers feed a runbook index that routes to three runbook categories, with a five-step maintenance loop at the bottom

content.text: The diagram shows six incident trigger types feeding a runbook router, which dispatches to three runbook categories: LLM/model issues, tool/integration failures, and agent behaviour issues. Below, the five-step maintenance loop — post-incident review, update runbook, peer review, version and publish, drill/simulate — keeps runbooks current.
When to Use It
Any agentic AI system operating in production with an on-call rotation — even a single engineer needs runbooks for failure modes they have not seen before.
After every model version upgrade — new models have different failure modes and refusal patterns that require runbook updates.
When expanding the agent's tool set — each new tool integration is a new class of integration failure.
When onboarding new on-call engineers — runbooks serve as the primary structured training material for incident response.
When SLA commitments require sub-30-minute resolution for critical failures — runbooks are the primary lever for reducing MTTR.
How to Implement
Step 1: Runbook Template Structure

# Runbook: [Symptom Name]

**Version**: 1.3  
**Last updated**: 2026-07-01  
**Owner**: @ml-platform-oncall  
**Applies to**: agent-service v2.x  

## Trigger
This runbook fires when:
- PagerDuty alert: `agent_error_rate_high` (>5% errors over 5 min)
- OR user escalation via support ticket tagged `ai-quality-regression`

## Severity
| Condition | Severity |
|-----------|----------|
| Error rate 5–20% | P2 — resolve within 4 hours |
| Error rate >20%  | P1 — resolve within 1 hour |

## Step 1: Collect Initial Context
```bash
# Get error rate by error type for the past 30 minutes
datadog-api --query 'sum:agent.errors{*} by {error_type}' --from -30m

# Get last 100 error log lines
aws logs filter-log-events \
  --log-group-name /prod/agent-service \
  --filter-pattern 'level=ERROR' \
  --start-time $(date -d '30 minutes ago' +%s000)
```

## Step 2: Classify the Error
- If errors contain `anthropic.BadRequestError`: → Step 3a (Context window)
- If errors contain `anthropic.RateLimitError`: → Step 3b (Rate limit)
- If errors contain `ToolCallError` or `schema validation failed`: → Step 3c (Tool schema)
- If agent logs show repeated identical actions: → Step 3d (Loop detection)

## Step 3a: Context Window Exceeded
```python
# Check P95 input token count for the past hour
# If P95 > 180k tokens, the conversation history is not being truncated.
# Apply the emergency prompt: add 'SUMMARIZE: ...' before sending to Claude.
```
Action: trim conversation history to last 10 turns; set `max_tokens` guard.  
Escalate to: `#ml-platform` if error rate does not drop within 15 minutes.

## Escalation
If unresolved after 30 minutes, page the ML platform lead.
Attach: error logs, last 5 affected conversation IDs, reproduction steps.

## Post-Incident
After resolution: update this runbook with any new diagnostic steps discovered.
File a post-mortem in Confluence: `Space > Incidents > [date]-[runbook-name]`.
Step 2: Automated Runbook Link in PagerDuty Alerts
Step 3: Agent Kill Switch Implementation

import redis
import logging
from functools import wraps

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
redis_client = redis.Redis(host="redis.prod.internal", port=6379, db=0)

# Kill switch key — set this in Redis to halt all agents
KILL_SWITCH_KEY = "agent:kill_switch"
KILL_SWITCH_AGENT_PREFIX = "agent:kill:"

def check_kill_switch(agent_id: str) -> bool:
    """Return True if this agent or all agents have been killed."""
    global_kill = redis_client.exists(KILL_SWITCH_KEY)
    agent_kill = redis_client.exists(f"{KILL_SWITCH_AGENT_PREFIX}{agent_id}")
    return bool(global_kill or agent_kill)

def with_kill_switch(func):
    """Decorator: abort the agent step if the kill switch is set."""
    @wraps(func)
    async def wrapper(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if check_kill_switch(self.agent_id):
            logger.warning("Kill switch active for agent %s — aborting step", self.agent_id)
            return {"status": "aborted", "reason": "kill_switch"}
        return await func(self, *args, **kwargs)
    return wrapper

# Operator commands (run during incident to stop a runaway agent):
# redis-cli SET agent:kill_switch 1 EX 3600     # kill ALL agents for 1 hour
# redis-cli SET agent:kill:agent-xyz 1 EX 3600  # kill specific agent
# redis-cli DEL agent:kill_switch               # restore normal operation
Examples
Example 1: Hallucination Detection and Runbook Step

import anthropic

def verify_agent_response(
    user_query: str,
    agent_response: str,
    retrieved_context: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    Cross-check an agent's response against the retrieved context
    to detect potential hallucinations.
    Returns a verdict and confidence score.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    prompt = f"""You are a fact-checker.

The agent was asked: {user_query}

The agent's response: {agent_response}

The only source the agent had access to:
{retrieved_context}

Does the agent's response contain any claims that are NOT supported by the source?
Reply with:
- SUPPORTED: all claims are directly supported by the source
- UNSUPPORTED: [list specific unsupported claims]
- AMBIGUOUS: [explain]"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )

    verdict_text = response.content[0].text
    return {
        "verdict": "SUPPORTED" if verdict_text.startswith("SUPPORTED") else "UNSUPPORTED",
        "detail": verdict_text,
    }
Example 2: Loop Detection in Agent Execution

from collections import deque
from typing import Optional
import hashlib

class LoopDetector:
    """Detect when an agent is repeating the same actions in a cycle."""

    def __init__(self, window_size: int = 10, max_repeat_fraction: float = 0.5):
        self.window_size = window_size
        self.max_repeat_fraction = max_repeat_fraction
        self._history: deque = deque(maxlen=window_size)

    def _action_hash(self, action: dict) -> str:
        # Hash the tool name + arguments to detect repeated identical calls
        payload = f"{action.get('tool')}:{sorted(action.get('args', {}).items())}"
        return hashlib.md5(payload.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]

    def check(self, action: dict) -> Optional[str]:
        """
        Record an action and return a warning message if a loop is detected.
        Returns None if no loop is detected.
        """
        h = self._action_hash(action)
        self._history.append(h)

        if len(self._history) < self.window_size:
            return None

        repeat_fraction = self._history.count(h) / len(self._history)
        if repeat_fraction >= self.max_repeat_fraction:
            return (
                f"Loop detected: action '{action.get('tool')}' repeated "
                f"{self._history.count(h)}/{len(self._history)} times in last "
                f"{self.window_size} steps."
            )
        return None

# Usage in an agent execution loop
detector = LoopDetector(window_size=10, max_repeat_fraction=0.5)

for step in agent_steps:
    warning = detector.check(step.action)
    if warning:
        logger.error(warning)
        raise AgentLoopError(warning)  # triggers the kill switch runbook
Real-World Example

GitHub Copilot's engineering team maintains runbooks for a family of model-serving failures specific to LLM inference: high-latency buckets (P99 > 30s), malformed output (JSON parse failures), context overflow, and safety filter false positives. Each runbook is linked directly from their PagerDuty alert configuration and includes Datadog query snippets for immediate log collection. After a 2024 incident where a model update caused a 20% increase in JSON parse failures, the team updated the relevant runbook within 24 hours and added an automated pre-deployment check that validates the model's output format against a suite of test cases before the runbook's specific failure mode can occur again — an example of runbooks driving preventive action, not just reactive recovery.
Common Mistakes
Writing runbooks after the incident has been resolved from memory — post-incident runbooks miss the intermediate diagnostic steps that led to the discovery.
Runbooks with commands that reference tools or environments that no longer exist — an on-call engineer running a stale
kubectl
command against a deprecated cluster wastes time they do not have.
No kill switch — every agentic system needs an emergency stop mechanism, and the runbook must reference it. An agent running without a kill switch in production is not production-ready.
Runbooks that describe what to do but not why — engineers following steps without understanding the logic cannot adapt when the situation does not exactly match.
No fire drills — runbooks that have never been executed against a simulated failure will fail when first used in a real incident.

Treating hallucination debugging like a bug report — you cannot reproduce a hallucination by rerunning the same input. Runbooks need to include probabilistic diagnostic steps, not deterministic reproduction instructions.
Best Practices
Link every PagerDuty alert directly to the relevant runbook in the alert annotation — the engineer should never have to search for it.
Keep runbooks in version control (Git) with a dated changelog — so you can see exactly what changed between the last successful drill and the current version.
Include executable commands in runbooks — copy-pasteable
aws logs
or
curl
commands are faster than narrative descriptions of what to check.
Run quarterly fire drills where engineers simulate incidents and follow the runbook against a staging environment — note any steps that fail or are confusing.
Update runbooks within 48 hours of a production incident — not after the post-mortem, but immediately while the steps are fresh.
Scope runbooks by symptom, not by component — an engineer sees an error message, not a component name, and the router must match the symptom they observe.
Add a "what this runbook does NOT cover" section at the top to prevent engineers from following it for the wrong failure class.
Performance and Scalability

Runbook maintenance scales with team size and the pace of system changes, not with traffic volume. The maintenance burden grows when the system adds new tools, upgrades model versions, or expands to new deployment environments — each of these is a trigger for a runbook review. To keep maintenance manageable, automate the reminder: link runbook review to the product changelog, the model upgrade pipeline, and the new tool onboarding checklist so the review is triggered automatically rather than relying on engineers to remember.
Automate runbook staleness detection: flag runbooks whose commands reference deprecated tools, old model versions, or retired endpoints.
Assign a runbook owner for each symptom category — not the whole library to one person.
Track runbook utilization: which runbooks are used most often? Those are the ones that need the highest maintenance priority.
Use a shared incident response channel where runbook gaps are reported in real time during incidents.
Tooling and Ecosystem
PagerDuty
— alert routing and on-call scheduling with runbook URL annotations in alert payloads.
Confluence / Notion
— wiki platforms for hosting runbooks with version history and search.
GitHub / GitLab
— version control for runbooks stored as Markdown, with PR review workflow for changes.
Datadog
— monitors and dashboards that provide the query snippets referenced in runbooks.
Opsgenie
— alternative to PagerDuty with built-in runbook linking and alert enrichment.
LangSmith / Helicone
— LLM observability platforms that log model inputs, outputs, token counts, and latency — the primary data sources for agent troubleshooting.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
MTTR
Junior-engineer usable
Maintenance cost
Maintained runbook library (this guide)
Low (minutes to find steps)
Yes
Medium — ongoing after each incident
Ad-hoc debugging (no runbooks)
High (hours of investigation)
No — requires expert knowledge
None
Post-mortem documents only
Medium (must read narrative)
Partially
Low
Automated remediation (no runbooks)
Very low (automated)
N/A — no human involved
High — must cover all failure modes
Security and Reliability
Restrict write access to runbooks to the on-call team and senior engineers — a runbook modified by an attacker during an incident to send engineers down wrong paths is a high-impact attack vector.
Do not include production credentials or API keys in runbooks — link to the secrets manager path where they can be retrieved under normal access controls.
The kill switch must have a faster activation path than the runbook — a Redis
SET
command that can be run in under 10 seconds, not a multi-step configuration change.
Log all runbook activations — when an engineer follows a runbook and it results in a restart or configuration change, that action must appear in the audit trail.
Test the kill switch in every quarterly fire drill — not just the diagnosis steps, but the actual activation and deactivation of the kill switch.
Key Takeaways
Agentic AI failures — hallucinations, loops, tool errors, prompt injection — are novel failure modes that require specialized runbooks, not generic server-down playbooks.
Organize runbooks by symptom category (LLM failures, tool failures, behaviour failures) with a top-level router that maps alert names and symptoms to runbooks.
Every agentic system must have a kill switch — a fast-activation emergency stop that can halt all agents or a specific agent within seconds.
Update runbooks within 48 hours of every production incident — not after the post-mortem is written, but while the diagnostic steps are fresh.
Run quarterly fire drills against a staging environment to validate that runbooks are executable — unexercised runbooks rot silently.
Link every PagerDuty alert directly to its runbook — the on-call engineer should have the runbook URL in the alert body, not have to search for it.
Include executable commands in runbooks, not just narrative descriptions — copy-pasteable log queries and API calls reduce time-to-diagnosis under pressure.
